policy_statement: Encourage rainwater harvesting to augment potable and irrigation water needs.
mechanism_description: Encouraging rainwater harvesting increases use of captured rainfall for irrigation and other needs, reducing demand on potable water supplies and improving water supply resilience.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (drought/variable rainfall) → ↑ distributed water storage/supply diversity → ↓ water-supply vulnerability (↓ exposure/sensitivity) → ↓ climate risk
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Human/system change: Buildings/sites install and use rainwater capture, storage, and non-potable distribution (e.g., for irrigation/toilets), reducing reliance on centralized potable supply. (2) Mediating process: Captured precipitation is stored and available during dry periods or interruptions, smoothing variability and reducing peak demand on potable networks. (3) Climate-relevant outcome: Improved water-supply resilience to drought and climate-driven precipitation variability; secondarily, potential reductions in energy use and GHGs from decreased potable water treatment and pumping.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced exposure to drought and water-supply disruption by diversifying and buffering water sources through onsite rain capture and storage.
mechanism_classification_reasoning: 1) Dominant causal pathway: address drought/precipitation variability by increasing localized water capture and storage, lowering vulnerability of water supply systems. 2) Why Adaptation over alternatives: while potable-demand reduction can save energy (a mitigation/resource-efficiency co-benefit), the core climate-relevant effect across instances is increased reliability/robustness of water supply under climate stress. 3) Edge cases applied: if implemented explicitly to cut energy use/utility emissions, mitigation could become primary at the policy level, but that is not inherent to the mechanism description here. 4) Mechanism-wide applicability: any policy framed as rainwater harvesting to reduce potable demand and improve supply resilience shares this adaptation-dominant pathway. 5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., mitigation co-benefits via reduced pumping/treatment) is deferred to policy-level evidence and local energy/water system characteristics.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: In water systems where treatment/pumping energy is very high (or where rainwater substitutes desalinated water), GHG reductions could be large; however, the mechanism as stated explicitly includes “water supply resilience,” making adaptation the consistent primary category across policies sharing it.
key_indicators: "Rain Water Harvesting"; "Encouraging rainwater harvesting"; "augment the need for potable and irrigation water"
co_benefits: Reduced stormwater runoff/flooding and drainage burden; potential energy/GHG reductions from lowered potable water treatment and pumping.
instance_edge_case_notes: “Encouraging” is ambiguous about the delivery mechanism (could be information/outreach or incentives), but no rebates, mandates, or standards are specified; classified as a general strategy/enabling measure.
